 {"id":"bd-2bwc","title":"Unit Tests: Update System (No Mocks)","description":"Add comprehensive unit tests for update system without mock infrastructure.

## Target Files (1 file, 0% coverage)
- rch/src/update/verify.rs - Update verification logic

## Related Files (already have some tests)
- rch/src/update/check.rs - Has basic tests
- rch/src/update/install.rs - Has basic tests

## Test Requirements
1. **Signature Verification**
   - Test valid signature acceptance
   - Test invalid signature rejection
   - Test missing signature handling
   - Test key rotation scenarios

2. **Checksum Verification**
   - Test SHA256 checksum matching
   - Test checksum format parsing
   - Test partial download detection

3. **Version Comparison**
   - Test semver comparison
   - Test pre-release handling
   - Test build metadata

## Constraints
- NO MockHTTP or fake downloads
- Use real test binaries (small)
- Test actual crypto verification
- Use deterministic test data

## Logging Requirements
- Each test: TEST START/TEST PASS format
- Log verification steps and results
- Include hex dumps on checksum failures","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:59:29.847124477Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T23:01:59.758927103Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2bwc","depends_on_id":"bd-1aim","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-25T23:01:59.758823739Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

Keep the unit user-level.","acceptance_criteria":"systemd unit includes Wants=network-online.target and After=network-online.target; no regression to worker mode.","notes":"User services still start after login; this change helps at boot or on slow network bring-up.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:02:23.729654792Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T22:08:56.872878729Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T22:08:56.872860304Z","close_reason":"Updated systemd unit","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-2csx","title":"README: mention optional daemon + fail-open","description":"Add a concise sentence near the quick-install block clarifying that rchd can run in the background but RCH always fails open to local builds if the daemon or worker pool is unavailable. This sets the right expectation for seamless behavior.","acceptance_criteria":"README mentions optional background daemon and explicit fail-open local fallback near the quick install block.","notes":"Keep wording short; this is a promise about safety and seamlessness.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:02:43.368712744Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T22:09:11.465959588Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T22:09:11.465936474Z","close_reason":"README updated","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-2df5","title":"Idea: Stable remote path per project when hash unchanged","description":"## Background
Remote path currently includes project hash and may churn; repeated builds with unchanged inputs should reuse the same remote path to improve cache hits and avoid redundant rsync dir creation.

## Goals
- Use a stable remote path for a project when its hash is unchanged.
- Keep isolation when hash changes (avoid mixing incompatible states).

## Design / Approach
- Maintain `project_id + hash` mapping but re-use existing remote dir if present.
- Add a small local cache file to remember last hash per project and worker (optional).
- Ensure cleanup routines respect stable paths.

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Adjust remote path computation to check for existing directory on worker.
- Add optional local cache metadata for speed.
- Update cleanup logic to avoid deleting active cache.

## Tests
- Unit: remote path selection logic.
- Integration: repeated build reuses remote dir.
- E2E: hash change triggers new remote dir.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Repeated builds with unchanged inputs reuse remote path.
- Hash changes still isolate build state.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-2df5.sh (one script per bead unless intentionally combined).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- Stability is per-worker; do not reuse across workers.
- If remote dir existence check fails, fall back to normal path.
- Old hash dirs should be eligible for cleanup to prevent bloat.

## E2E Outline
- Same hash builds reuse same remote dir.
- Change key file -> new hash -> new remote dir.
- Cleanup keeps newest dir.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rch/src/transfer.rs: remote path computation with hash reuse.
- cleanup logic ignores active hash dir.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-2df5.sh: same hash -> reuse; different hash -> new dir.

 {"id":"bd-2ga8","title":"True E2E Output Validation & Correctness Tests","description":"# Feature: True E2E Output Validation & Correctness Tests

## Purpose

Test that output from remote compilation (stdout, stderr, exit codes, terminal colors) is correctly preserved and propagated to the calling agent.

## MANDATORY Logging Requirements

Output validation tests MUST use structured JSON logging:

### Output-Specific Log Events

```json
// Output capture start
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"test_exit_code_101","phase":"execute","msg":"Command executed","data":{"cmd":"cargo test","mode":"remote","worker":"css"}}

// Exit code verification
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"test_exit_code_101","phase":"verify","msg":"Exit code check","data":{"expected":101,"actual":101,"match":true,"semantic":"tests_failed"}}

// stdout/stderr comparison
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"test_stderr_preservation","phase":"verify","msg":"Output comparison","data":{"stream":"stderr","local_bytes":1234,"remote_bytes":1234,"match":true,"hash_local":"abc123","hash_remote":"abc123"}}

// Color code detection
{"ts":"...","level":"DEBUG","test":"test_ansi_colors","phase":"verify","msg":"ANSI codes detected","data":{"total_codes":15,"colors":["red","green","yellow"],"styles":["bold","underline"]}}

// Streaming latency
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"test_streaming","phase":"timing","msg":"First byte latency","data":{"local_first_byte_ms":50,"remote_first_byte_ms":85,"overhead_ms":35}}

// Mismatch details (on failure)
{"ts":"...","level":"ERROR","test":"test_output_mismatch","phase":"verify","msg":"Output mismatch detected","data":{"stream":"stdout","line":42,"local":"expected text","remote":"actual text","diff":"<unified diff snippet>"}}
```

### Metrics to Log

1. **Exit codes**: expected, actual, semantic meaning
2. **Output size**: bytes per stream, truncation if any
3. **Content hash**: SHA-256 of local vs remote
4. **ANSI codes**: detected codes, colors, styles
5. **Timing**: first byte latency, total duration
6. **Diff details**: on mismatch, show context

## Why This Matters

- AI agents parse compilation output to understand errors and fix code
- Corrupted or missing output makes agents unable to diagnose issues
- Terminal colors help agents distinguish warnings from errors
- Exit codes determine whether agents retry or report failure

## Output Types to Verify

### Exit Code Semantics

1. **Exit 0** = success (all tests passed, build succeeded)
   - Log: semantic="success", verify stdout contains success indicators
   
2. **Exit 1** = compilation/build error
   - Log: semantic="build_error", verify error message in stderr
   
3. **Exit 101** = cargo test: tests ran but some failed
   - Log: semantic="tests_failed", count failed tests in output
   
4. **Exit 128+N** = killed by signal N
   - Log: signal number, signal name
   
5. **All non-zero** denies local re-execution
   - Log: verify no local fallback on compilation error

### stdout/stderr Preservation

1. **Compiler error messages** (line numbers, context)
   - Log: line number parsed correctly, context preserved
   
2. **Test output** (pass/fail indicators, assertion messages)
   - Log: test count, pass/fail breakdown
   
3. **Warning messages** (not lost or mixed)
   - Log: warning count, no interleaving corruption
   
4. **Progress indicators** (cargo build percentage)
   - Log: progress updates received
   
5. **Multi-line output** (stack traces, long errors)
   - Log: line count, no truncation

### Terminal Formatting

1. **ANSI color codes preserved** (TERM, NO_COLOR, CARGO_TERM_COLOR)
   - Log: TERM value, color codes detected, preservation status
   
2. **Bold/underline for emphasis**
   - Log: style codes found
   
3. **Color-coded test results** (green pass, red fail)
   - Log: pass=green, fail=red verification
   
4. **cargo's progress bar and spinner**
   - Log: carriage returns, spinner frames
   
5. **Wide characters and unicode**
   - Log: unicode points found, correct rendering

### Streaming Behavior

1. **Output streams in real-time** (not batched at end)
   - Log: timestamp of first byte, timestamp of last byte
   
2. **stderr and stdout interleaved correctly**
   - Log: interleaving order preserved
   
3. **Large output (>1MB) handled** without truncation
   - Log: total bytes, no truncation marker
   
4. **Binary data in output** doesn't corrupt stream
   - Log: binary bytes detected, stream integrity

## Merged E2E Scenarios (Hook/Feature-Flag/Error Display)

### Hook Protocol Integrity (from bd-3obh)

1. **Hook JSON response remains pristine**
   - Run: `echo '{"tool":"Bash","input":{"command":"cargo build"}}' | rch hook --stdin`
   - Verify stdout is valid JSON (no ANSI), stderr only for rich output
   - Log: json_valid=true, ansi_present=false, stderr_bytes

2. **Compilation output passthrough**
   - Run: `rch compile cargo build` on a fixture with known output
   - Verify stdout/stderr identical to local execution (byte-for-byte after normalization)
   - Log: stdout/stderr hashes, diff on mismatch

### Rich Output Gating & Feature Flags (from bd-dej3)

1. **Compile-time rich-ui off**
   - Build without rich-ui feature flag
   - Verify no rich formatting emitted anywhere
   - Log: build_flags, ansi_present=false

2. **Runtime gating**
   - With rich-ui on, ensure `--json` output is still plain JSON and stderr-only rich output
   - With `NO_COLOR=1`, ensure ANSI stripped
   - Log: TERM/NO_COLOR values and ansi detection

### Error Display Verification (from bd-vp67)

1. **SSH connection refused display**
   - Expected: error code, host/port, remediation hint
   - Verify NO_COLOR removes ANSI

2. **Config parse error display**
   - Show file path, line number, and snippet context

3. **Build failure display**
   - Compiler error text preserved verbatim

4. **JSON error mode**
   - `--json` outputs structured error to stdout
   - Error object includes code + message + remediation (if available)

5. **Severity differentiation**
   - Warning vs error output visually distinct (icons/text), no crash

6. **stderr-only guarantee**
   - Non-JSON errors never pollute stdout (stdout empty or valid JSON)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Exit codes match local execution
- [ ] stdout/stderr byte-for-byte identical (after normalization)
- [ ] Colors preserved when TERM is set
- [ ] Streaming works for long builds
- [ ] No output corruption for any test case
- [ ] Hook JSON responses are valid and ANSI-free
- [ ] Rich output never contaminates stdout in --json mode
- [ ] Error displays include code + remediation and respect NO_COLOR
- [ ] ALL tests emit structured JSON logs
- [ ] Mismatches logged with detailed diffs

## Technical Notes

- Use std::process::Output for capture
- Test with various TERM values (xterm-256color, dumb, etc)
- Verify against local execution baseline
- Log raw byte sequences for debugging

## Dependencies

- Requires bd-3saj (test infrastructure)
- Requires bd-12hi (cargo compilation produces output to verify)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-19T18:21:13.741339923Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-26T00:22:25.446584029Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2ga8","depends_on_id":"bd-12hi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T18:21:21.565575304Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2ga8","depends_on_id":"bd-1cwg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T18:21:19.052702518Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2ga8","depends_on_id":"bd-3saj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T18:21:20.652641255Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2h45","title":"Add --schema flag to emit JSON Schema for outputs","description":"Add a --schema flag that emits JSON Schema for the tool's JSON output format. This enables agents to validate output and understand structure programmatically.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T05:42:46.047119740Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T05:42:46.047119740Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-2hbf","title":"Idea: Worker load-average guard","description":"## Background
Even if slots are available, a worker may be overloaded (load avg spikes), which hurts build times. A load guard improves selection quality.

## Goals
- Skip workers whose load average exceeds a threshold.
- Use real telemetry when available; fall back to SSH `uptime` parsing.

## Design / Approach
- Add load_avg_1/5/15 to worker telemetry/capabilities.
- Add selection threshold: selection.max_load_per_core.
- Integrate into selection filter and status output.

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Extend telemetry payload or SSH probe to capture load avg.
- Add config and validation.
- Update selection logic + reason codes.

## Tests
- Unit: selection filters high-load workers.
- Integration: mock telemetry with load > threshold.
- E2E: status output displays load.

## Acceptance Criteria
- High-load workers are skipped.
- Threshold is configurable and documented.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-2hbf.sh (one script per bead unless intentionally combined).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- If all workers exceed threshold, pick least-loaded with warning (fail-open).
- Missing load telemetry -> allow worker but warn.
- Use load-per-core to normalize across machines.

## E2E Outline
- Mixed load -> skip high, select low.
- All high -> select least-loaded with warning.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rchd/src/selection.rs: load-per-core threshold logic.
- rchd/src/telemetry.rs: load avg parsing.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-2hbf.sh: mixed load -> select low; all high -> warn + pick least.

Load average guard merged with disk space guard.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2hbf","depends_on_id":"bd-155i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:57:53.077759297Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3u68","title":"Create RchConsole wrapper with context-aware output","description":"# Create RchConsole wrapper with context-aware output

## Task Description

Create rch/src/ui/console.rs with an RchConsole struct that wraps rich_rusts Console and automatically respects OutputContext. This is the primary interface for all rich output in RCH.

## Background

We need a wrapper because:
1. Raw Console doesnt know about our context detection
2. We need fallback methods for non-rich contexts
3. We want consistent behavior across all commands
4. We need to route output to stderr (not stdout)

## Implementation

```rust
// rch/src/ui/console.rs

use rich_rust::prelude::*;
use rch_common::ui::context::OutputContext;
use rch_common::ui::theme::RchTheme;
use std::io::{self, Write};

/// Context-aware console wrapper that respects output mode
///
/// This is the primary interface for rich output in RCH commands.
/// It automatically:
/// - Detects output context (interactive, piped, hook, etc.)
/// - Routes output to stderr (stdout reserved for data)
/// - Falls back to plain text when appropriate
/// - Respects NO_COLOR and FORCE_COLOR
pub struct RchConsole {
    /// The underlying rich_rust Console
    inner: Console,

    /// Detected output context
    context: OutputContext,
}

impl RchConsole {
    /// Create a new RchConsole with automatic context detection
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        let context = OutputContext::detect();
        let inner = Self::build_console(context);
        Self { inner, context }
    }

    /// Create with explicit context (for testing)
    pub fn with_context(context: OutputContext) -> Self {
        let inner = Self::build_console(context);
        Self { inner, context }
    }

    fn build_console(context: OutputContext) -> Console {
        Console::builder()
            .force_terminal(context.supports_rich())
            // Output to stderr by default for rich content
            // stdout is reserved for machine-readable data
            .build()
    }

    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
    // CONTEXT QUERIES
    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

    /// Check if rich output is available
    pub fn is_rich(&self) -> bool {
        self.context.supports_rich()
    }

    /// Check if colors are available
    pub fn is_colored(&self) -> bool {
        self.context.supports_color()
    }

    /// Check if in machine output mode
    pub fn is_machine(&self) -> bool {
        self.context.is_machine()
    }

    /// Get the detected context
    pub fn context(&self) -> OutputContext {
        self.context
    }

    /// Get terminal width (or default 80)
    pub fn width(&self) -> usize {
        self.inner.width()
    }

    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
    // RICH OUTPUT METHODS (only work in rich mode)
    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

    /// Print with markup parsing (only if interactive)
    ///
    /// Does nothing in machine/plain modes.
    pub fn print_rich(&self, content: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print(content);
        }
    }

    /// Print a renderable (table, panel, etc.)
    ///
    /// Does nothing in machine/plain modes.
    pub fn print_renderable<R: Renderable>(&self, renderable: &R) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print_renderable(renderable);
        }
    }

    /// Print a horizontal rule
    ///
    /// Falls back to dashes in non-rich modes.
    pub fn rule(&self, title: Option<&str>) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.rule(title);
        } else if !self.context.is_machine() {
            // Plain fallback
            let width = 60;
            if let Some(t) = title {
                let padding = (width - t.len() - 2) / 2;
                eprintln!("{} {} {}", "-".repeat(padding), t, "-".repeat(padding));
            } else {
                eprintln!("{}", "-".repeat(width));
            }
        }
    }

    /// Print a blank line
    pub fn line(&self) {
        if !self.context.is_machine() {
            self.inner.line();
        }
    }

    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
    // FALLBACK OUTPUT METHODS
    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

    /// Print with rich/plain fallback
    ///
    /// In rich mode: prints rich content
    /// In plain mode: prints plain content
    /// In machine mode: prints nothing
    pub fn print_or_plain(&self, rich: &str, plain: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print(rich);
        } else if !self.context.is_machine() {
            eprintln!("{}", plain);
        }
    }

    /// Print plain text to stderr (always, unless machine mode)
    ///
    /// Use for messages that should appear in plain mode too.
    pub fn print_plain(&self, content: &str) {
        if !self.context.is_machine() {
            eprintln!("{}", content);
        }
    }

    /// Print error with rich panel or plain fallback
    ///
    /// Always prints (errors should show even in machine mode to stderr).
    pub fn print_error(&self, title: &str, message: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            let panel = Panel::from_text(message)
                .title(&format!("✗ {}", title))
                .border_style(RchTheme::error())
                .rounded();
            self.inner.print_renderable(&panel);
        } else {
            eprintln!("Error: {}", title);
            eprintln!("{}", message);
        }
    }

    /// Print success message
    pub fn print_success(&self, message: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print(&format!("[bold green]✓[/] {}", message));
        } else if !self.context.is_machine() {
            eprintln!("[OK] {}", message);
        }
    }

    /// Print warning message
    pub fn print_warning(&self, message: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print(&format!("[bold yellow]⚠[/] {}", message));
        } else if !self.context.is_machine() {
            eprintln!("[WARN] {}", message);
        }
    }

    /// Print info message
    pub fn print_info(&self, message: &str) {
        if self.context.supports_rich() {
            self.inner.print(&format!("[bold blue]ℹ[/] {}", message));
        } else if !self.context.is_machine() {
            eprintln!("[INFO] {}", message);
        }
    }

    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
    // MACHINE OUTPUT (to stdout)
    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

    /// Print JSON to stdout (for --json flag)
    ///
    /// This goes to stdout, not stderr!
    pub fn print_json<T: serde::Serialize>(&self, value: &T) -> serde_json::Result<()> {
        let json = serde_json::to_string_pretty(value)?;
        println!("{}", json);
        Ok(())
    }

    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
    // ACCESS TO UNDERLYING CONSOLE
    // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

    /// Get the underlying rich_rust Console
    ///
    /// Use sparingly - prefer the wrapper methods.
    pub fn inner(&self) -> &Console {
        &self.inner
    }
}

impl Default for RchConsole {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::new()
    }
}

// ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
// STATIC INSTANCE FOR CONVENIENCE
// ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════

use once_cell::sync::Lazy;

/// Global console instance with automatic context detection
///
/// Use this for quick access in command handlers:
/// ```
/// use rch::ui::console::CONSOLE;
/// CONSOLE.print_success("Done!");
/// ```
pub static CONSOLE: Lazy<RchConsole> = Lazy::new(RchConsole::new);
```

## Usage Examples

```rust
use rch::ui::console::{RchConsole, CONSOLE};

// Using global instance
fn handle_status_command() {
    CONSOLE.print_rich("[bold]Daemon Status[/]");
    // ...
}

// Using local instance with explicit context
fn handle_with_context(json_mode: bool) {
    let ctx = if json_mode {
        OutputContext::Machine
    } else {
        OutputContext::detect()
    };
    let console = RchConsole::with_context(ctx);

    if console.is_machine() {
        console.print_json(&status)?;
    } else {
        console.print_renderable(&status_table);
    }
}
```

## Testing

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_machine_mode_no_output() {
        let console = RchConsole::with_context(OutputContext::Machine);
        // These should do nothing (not panic)
        console.print_rich("[bold]test[/]");
        console.print_plain("test");
        // Only print_json and print_error should work
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_plain_mode_no_markup() {
        let console = RchConsole::with_context(OutputContext::Plain);
        // Should work but without markup
        console.print_plain("test");
        console.print_or_plain("[bold]rich[/]", "plain");
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_context_queries() {
        let interactive = RchConsole::with_context(OutputContext::Interactive);
        assert!(interactive.is_rich());
        assert!(interactive.is_colored());
        assert!(!interactive.is_machine());

        let machine = RchConsole::with_context(OutputContext::Machine);
        assert!(!machine.is_rich());
        assert!(machine.is_machine());
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. [ ] RchConsole wraps Console with context awareness
2. [ ] print_rich() only outputs in interactive mode
3. [ ] print_plain() outputs in non-machine modes
4. [ ] print_error() always outputs (errors are important)
5. [ ] print_json() outputs to stdout (not stderr)
6. [ ] Global CONSOLE instance works
7. [ ] Unit tests pass

-{"id":"bd-5a5k","title":"Idea: Project affinity pinning window","description":"Idea: Project affinity pinning + warm-cache + last-success fallback

## Background
Cache affinity helps incremental builds, but selection can still bounce between workers. A short-lived pin improves cache hit rates without starving other workers. Warm-cache pre-sync reduces first-build latency. A last-success fallback improves resilience when selection yields no match.

## Goals
- Pin a project to the last successful worker for a configurable window (e.g., 30-120 minutes).
- Add warm-cache command to pre-sync sources without executing builds.
- If selection yields no worker, fall back to last-successful worker if healthy.

## Design / Approach
- Store last-successful worker + timestamp in daemon history.
- Selection checks pin window before normal strategy.
- If selection fails, attempt last-successful worker (health/slots/runtime OK).
- New CLI: `rch cache warm [--workers ...] [--project PATH]`.
- Reuse TransferPipeline sync_to_remote without execute step.
- Record warm-cache events for affinity tracking.
- Config knobs:
  - selection.affinity_pin_minutes
  - selection.enable_last_success_fallback (bool)

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Add pin metadata + last-success mapping to build history.
- Implement selection override with safeguards (pin + fallback).
- Implement warm-cache command path (sync-only).
- Emit selection audit reasons (pinned, fallback, normal).

## Tests
- Unit: pin expiry logic.
- Unit: fallback logic with unhealthy/insufficient runtime.
- Unit: warm-cache command argument parsing.
- Integration: selection respects pin window and fallback.
- E2E: warm-cache improves subsequent build affinity selection.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pinning improves cache hit rates for repeated builds.
- Warm-cache completes without running builds.
- Fallback improves success rate without masking real errors.
- Behavior is transparent in logs/status.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-5a5k.sh (combined: affinity + warm-cache + fallback).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- Pin respects health/runtime requirements; skip if unhealthy.
- Pin window expiration is enforced.
- Fallback only if worker healthy, runtime OK, and slots available.
- Warm-cache is idempotent; failures on one worker do not abort others.
- Force_local bypasses pin and fallback entirely.

## E2E Outline
- Warm-cache a worker -> rsync only, no exec.
- Next build prefers warmed worker (affinity).
- After pin expiry -> normal selection resumes.
- Force selection failure -> fallback uses last-success worker.
- Unhealthy fallback -> no selection and clear reason.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rchd/src/selection.rs: pinning window + expiry + fallback logic.
- rchd/src/history.rs: last-successful mapping accuracy.
- rch/src/commands.rs: CLI parsing for cache warm flags.
- rch/src/transfer.rs: sync-only (no exec) path.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-5a5k.sh: warm-cache, verify no exec; affinity next build; fallback when selection fails.

+{"id":"bd-5a5k","title":"Project Cache Affinity System (pinning + warm-cache + stable paths)","description":"# Project Cache Affinity System

## Merged Scope
This bead consolidates cache affinity features for improved build performance:
- **Affinity Pinning Window** (original bd-5a5k): Pin projects to successful workers
- **Warm-Cache Command**: Pre-sync sources without executing builds
- **Last-Success Fallback**: Fall back to last successful worker when selection fails
- **Stable Remote Paths** (merged from bd-2df5): Reuse remote paths when hash unchanged

All these features work together to maximize cache hit rates and minimize redundant transfers.

## Background
Cache affinity helps incremental builds, but several factors can reduce effectiveness:
- Selection can bounce between workers
- Remote paths may churn even for unchanged projects
- First build to a worker suffers cold-cache latency
- No fallback when preferred worker is unavailable

This unified system addresses all these issues holistically.

## Goals
1. Pin a project to the last successful worker for a configurable window (30-120 min)
2. Add `rch cache warm` command to pre-sync sources without executing builds
3. If selection yields no worker, fall back to last-successful worker if healthy
4. Reuse stable remote path for a project when its hash is unchanged
5. Keep isolation when hash changes (avoid mixing incompatible states)

## Design / Approach

### Affinity Pinning
- Store last-successful worker + timestamp in daemon history
- Selection checks pin window before normal strategy
- Config: `selection.affinity_pin_minutes`

### Last-Success Fallback
- If selection fails, attempt last-successful worker (health/slots/runtime OK)
- Config: `selection.enable_last_success_fallback`

### Warm-Cache Command
```bash
rch cache warm [--workers ...] [--project PATH]
```
- Reuses TransferPipeline sync_to_remote without execute step
- Records warm-cache events for affinity tracking

### Stable Remote Paths
- Maintain `project_id + hash` mapping
- Reuse existing remote dir if hash unchanged
- Add optional local cache metadata for speed
- Cleanup routines respect stable paths

### Remote Path Computation
```
/tmp/rch/{project_id}/{hash}/
```
- If hash unchanged and dir exists on worker → reuse
- If hash changed → new directory
- Per-worker stability (not cross-worker)

## Tasks / Subtasks
1. Add pin metadata + last-success mapping to build history
2. Implement selection override with safeguards (pin + fallback)
3. Implement warm-cache command path (sync-only)
4. Adjust remote path computation to check for existing directory
5. Add local cache metadata for path stability tracking
6. Update cleanup logic to avoid deleting active cache
7. Emit selection audit reasons (pinned, fallback, normal)

## Tests

### Unit Tests
- `rchd/src/selection.rs`: pin expiry logic, fallback logic
- `rch/src/transfer.rs`: remote path computation with hash reuse
- `rch/src/commands.rs`: warm-cache argument parsing
- Cleanup logic ignores active hash dirs

### Integration Tests
- Selection respects pin window and fallback
- Repeated build reuses remote dir

### E2E Tests
Script: `scripts/e2e_cache_affinity.sh`

Scenarios:
- Same hash builds reuse same remote dir
- Change key file -> new hash -> new remote dir
- Warm-cache improves subsequent build selection
- Fallback succeeds when preferred worker down
- Cleanup keeps newest dir

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pinning improves cache hit rates for repeated builds
- Warm-cache completes without running builds
- Fallback improves success rate without masking real errors
- Repeated builds with unchanged inputs reuse remote path
- Hash changes still isolate build state
- Behavior is transparent in logs/status

## Logging & E2E
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result
- Include selection reason (pinned/fallback/normal) in logs

## Edge Cases & UX
- Stability is per-worker; do not reuse across workers
- If remote dir existence check fails, fall back to normal path
- Old hash dirs eligible for cleanup to prevent bloat
- Unhealthy/insufficient-runtime workers not used for fallback","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:55:22.989967781Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-26T00:41:02.143287080Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-5a5k","depends_on_id":"bd-3i0q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-25T23:42:48.633457489Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-5a5k","depends_on_id":"bd-zp4j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-25T23:42:57.423883789Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-785w","title":"Idea: Allowlist of executable commands for remote run","description":"## Background
Remote execution should be constrained to known safe compilers/build tools. An explicit allowlist reduces risk from unexpected command injection or misclassification edge cases.

## Goals
- Require the remote-exec command to match an allowlist (cargo/rustc/gcc/clang/bun test, etc.).
- Allow customization per project to include additional safe tools.

## Design / Approach
- Add config: execution.allowlist (default = current supported commands).
- Validate that classified command matches allowlist; otherwise fail-open (allow local only).
- Provide diagnostics in `rch diagnose`.

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Define default allowlist aligned with classifier.
- Add allowlist check in hook before remote execution.
- Add config validation + documentation.

## Tests
- Unit: allowlist matching for supported commands.
- Integration: unknown command is forced local.
- E2E: allowlist override enables custom tool.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Only allowlisted commands run remotely.
- Safe override via config and clear logging.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-785w.sh (one script per bead unless intentionally combined).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- Allowlist empty -> no remote execution (local only) with reason.
- Per-project allowlist merges with global (document precedence).
- Must still pass classifier and safety checks.

## E2E Outline
- Remove cargo from allowlist -> local allowed.
- Add cargo -> remote allowed.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rch-common/src/patterns.rs: allowlist matcher aligns with classifier.
- rch/src/hook.rs: allowlist blocks remote execution with reason.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-785w.sh: remove cargo from allowlist -> local allowed; add back -> remote.

 {"id":"bd-7ot6","title":"Create Test Fixture: Rust Project with Build Script (build.rs)","description":"## Purpose
Create a Rust project fixture with a custom build.rs build script for testing scenarios where build scripts generate code, link native libraries, or perform other build-time operations.

## Why This is Needed
Many real Rust projects use build.rs for:
- Code generation (protobuf, bindgen, etc.)
- Native library linking
- Environment variable configuration
- Conditional compilation

RCH must correctly handle projects where build.rs executes during compilation.

## Requirements
1. Project under tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs/
2. Custom build.rs that generates a source file
3. Main code that uses the generated file
4. Verify generated code works on both local and remote

## Structure
```
tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs/
├── Cargo.toml
├── build.rs          # Build script that generates code
└── src/
    └── main.rs       # Uses generated code via include!
```

Note: OUT_DIR is created by cargo during build, not committed.

## File Content Specifications

### Cargo.toml
```toml
[package]
name = "with_build_rs"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"
build = "build.rs"

[dependencies]
# No runtime dependencies needed for this simple fixture
```

### build.rs
```rust
//! Build script that generates version info and a greeting function.
//! This demonstrates common build.rs patterns used in real projects.

use std::env;
use std::fs;
use std::path::Path;

fn main() {
    // Get the output directory from cargo
    let out_dir = env::var("OUT_DIR").expect("OUT_DIR not set");
    let dest_path = Path::new(&out_dir).join("generated.rs");

    // Generate version info from environment
    let version = env::var("CARGO_PKG_VERSION").unwrap_or_else(|_| "unknown".to_string());
    let build_timestamp = chrono_like_timestamp();

    // Generate the Rust code
    let generated_code = format!(
        r#"// Auto-generated by build.rs - do not edit!

/// Returns version info generated at build time
pub fn version_info() -> &'static str {{
    "{version}"
}}

/// Returns the build timestamp
pub fn build_timestamp() -> &'static str {{
    "{build_timestamp}"
}}

/// A greeting generated at build time
pub fn generated_greeting() -> &'static str {{
    "Hello from build.rs generated code!"
}}

/// Demonstrates that build.rs ran successfully
pub const BUILD_RS_RAN: bool = true;
"#,
        version = version,
        build_timestamp = build_timestamp,
    );

    // Write the generated file
    fs::write(&dest_path, generated_code).expect("Failed to write generated.rs");

    // Tell cargo to rerun if build.rs changes
    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=build.rs");

    // Tell cargo this generates code
    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=CARGO_PKG_VERSION");
}

/// Simple timestamp without external dependencies
fn chrono_like_timestamp() -> String {
    // In a real project, you'd use chrono or time crate
    // For this fixture, we use a static string for reproducibility
    "2026-01-19T00:00:00Z".to_string()
}
```

### src/main.rs
```rust
//! Main binary that uses the build.rs generated code.
//! This demonstrates the include! pattern for generated modules.

// Include the generated code from OUT_DIR
include!(concat!(env!("OUT_DIR"), "/generated.rs"));

fn main() {
    println!("=== Build.rs Fixture Test ===");
    println!("Version: {}", version_info());
    println!("Build time: {}", build_timestamp());
    println!("Greeting: {}", generated_greeting());
    println!("build.rs ran: {}", BUILD_RS_RAN);
    println!("=== Test Complete ===");
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_version_info_not_empty() {
        assert!(!version_info().is_empty());
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_build_timestamp_not_empty() {
        assert!(!build_timestamp().is_empty());
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_generated_greeting() {
        assert_eq!(generated_greeting(), "Hello from build.rs generated code!");
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_build_rs_ran() {
        assert!(BUILD_RS_RAN, "build.rs should have set this to true");
    }
}
```

## Test Cases This Enables

### 1. Build with build.rs
- Command: `cargo build`
- Verify: build.rs executes (check for generated.rs in target dir)
- Verify: Binary compiles successfully
- Verify: Binary produces expected output
- Log: build.rs execution, generated file creation

### 2. Remote build
- Sync project to worker
- Run `cargo build` on worker
- Verify: OUT_DIR is created on worker
- Verify: Generated code compiles on worker
- Verify: Artifact returns and runs locally
- Log: remote OUT_DIR location, artifact retrieval

### 3. Incremental build - change build.rs
- Modify build.rs (e.g., change greeting)
- Run `cargo build` again
- Verify: build.rs reruns (cargo:rerun-if-changed)
- Verify: New generated code is used
- Log: rebuild trigger, regeneration

### 4. Clean build cycle
- Run `cargo clean`
- Run `cargo build`
- Verify: Full generation cycle works
- Log: clean state, full regeneration

## Validation Logging
```json
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"setup","msg":"Validating build.rs fixture","data":{"path":"fixtures/with_build_rs"}}
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"validate","msg":"Build script exists","data":{"file":"build.rs","exists":true,"lines":45}}
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"validate","msg":"Main source exists","data":{"file":"src/main.rs","exists":true,"has_include_macro":true}}
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"build","msg":"Building fixture","data":{"cmd":"cargo build"}}
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"validate","msg":"Generated code verified","data":{"out_dir_file":"generated.rs","exists":true,"content_valid":true}}
{"ts":"...","level":"INFO","test":"fixture_validation","phase":"execute","msg":"Binary execution test","data":{"exit_code":0,"output_contains":"build.rs ran: true"}}
```

## Unit Tests for Fixture

```rust
// tests/true_e2e/tests/build_rs_fixture_tests.rs

use std::process::Command;
use std::path::PathBuf;

#[test]
fn test_build_rs_fixture_compiles() {
    let fixture_path = PathBuf::from("tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs");
    let output = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["build"])
        .current_dir(&fixture_path)
        .output()
        .expect("cargo build failed");
    assert!(output.status.success(), "Build failed: {}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr));
}

#[test]
fn test_build_rs_generates_code() {
    let fixture_path = PathBuf::from("tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs");
    // Build first
    let _ = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["build"])
        .current_dir(&fixture_path)
        .output();

    // Check that generated.rs exists in OUT_DIR (inside target/debug/build/...)
    let build_dir = fixture_path.join("target/debug/build");
    assert!(build_dir.exists(), "Build directory should exist after build");

    // Find the generated file
    let has_generated = std::fs::read_dir(&build_dir)
        .unwrap()
        .filter_map(|e| e.ok())
        .any(|entry| {
            let path = entry.path();
            path.is_dir() && path.join("out/generated.rs").exists()
        });
    assert!(has_generated, "generated.rs should exist in OUT_DIR");
}

#[test]
fn test_build_rs_binary_output() {
    let fixture_path = PathBuf::from("tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs");
    let output = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["run"])
        .current_dir(&fixture_path)
        .output()
        .expect("cargo run failed");

    assert!(output.status.success());
    let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout);
    assert!(stdout.contains("build.rs ran: true"), "Output should confirm build.rs ran");
    assert!(stdout.contains("Hello from build.rs generated code!"), "Should have generated greeting");
}

#[test]
fn test_build_rs_tests_pass() {
    let fixture_path = PathBuf::from("tests/e2e/fixtures/with_build_rs");
    let output = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["test"])
        .current_dir(&fixture_path)
        .output()
        .expect("cargo test failed");

    assert!(output.status.success(), "Tests failed: {}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout));
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] build.rs executes during cargo build
- [ ] Generated code is included correctly via include! macro
- [ ] Remote build produces working binary
- [ ] Incremental builds regenerate on build.rs change
- [ ] OUT_DIR content not synced back (only final artifacts)
- [ ] All unit tests pass
